query: Are there papers that propose contextualized calibration for the probability of answers in language models?
criteria: contextualized calibration answer probabilities language models
calibration of answer probabilities in language models with context
context-aware probability calibration for LM outputs

authors_year.value: Zhengping Jiang, Anqi Liu, Benjamin Van Durme (2022)

recommendation_reason.value: We investigate model calibration in the setting of zero-shot cross-lingual transfer with largescale pre-trained language models.

supported_research_question.value: We investigate model calibration in the setting of zero-shot cross-lingual transfer with largescale pre-trained language models.

method.value: We investigate model calibration in the setting of zero-shot cross-lingual transfer with largescale pre-trained language models.

data_or_scenario.value: There exists an essential need for model calibration when natural language models are deployed in critical tasks.

main_conclusion.value: We find that models trained with data from the source language become less calibrated when applied to the target language and that calibration errors increase with intrinsic task difficulty and relative sparsity of training data.

limitations.value: Finally, our comparison demonstrates that among other methods Temperature Scaling (TS) generalizes well to distant languages, but TS fails to calibrate more complex confidence estimation in structured predictions compared to more expressive alternatives like Gaussian Process Calibration.

relevance_strength.value: We investigate model calibration in the setting of zero-shot cross-lingual transfer with largescale pre-trained language models.

title.value: Calibration of Machine Reading Systems at Scale

authors_year.value: Shehzaad Dhuliawala, Leonard Adolphs, Raj Das, Mrinmaya Sachan (2022)

source_or_doi.value: DOI: 10.18653/v1/2022.findings-acl.133; Source: Findings of the Association for Computational Linguistics: ACL 2022; OA: hybrid; Citations: 6

recommendation_reason.value: In typical machine learning systems, an estimate of the probability of the prediction is used to assess the system's confidence in the prediction. This confidence measure is usually uncalibrated; i.e. the system's confidence in the prediction does not match the true probability of the predicted output.

supported_research_question.value: In this paper, we present an investigation into calibrating open setting machine reading systems such as open-domain question answering and claim verification systems.

method.value: We propose simple extensions to existing calibration approaches that allow us to adapt these callibrators to these settings.

data_or_scenario.value: open-domain question answering and claim verification systems

main_conclusion.value: Our experimental results reveal that the joint callibration of the retriever and the reader outperforms the reader calibrator by a significant margin.

limitations.value: We show that calibrating such complex systems which contain a discrete retrieval and deep reading components is challenging and current calibration techniques fail to scale to these settings.

relevance_strength.value: We also show that the callibrator can be useful for selective prediction, e.g., when question answering systems are posed with unanswerable or out-ofthe-training distribution questions.

title_match.query_title: Calibration of Machine Reading Systems at Scale

authors_year.value: Clara Meister, Tiago Pimentel, Gian Wiher, Ryan Cotterell (2022)

source_or_doi.value: DOI: 10.3929/ethz-b-000597055; Source: arXiv (Cornell University); OA: green; Citations: 17

recommendation_reason.value: In this work, we posit that the abstraction of natural language generation as a discrete stochastic process-which allows for an information-theoretic analysis-can provide new insights into the behavior of probabilistic language generators, for example, why high-probability texts can be dull or repetitive.

supported_research_question.value: In this work, we posit that the abstraction of natural language generation as a discrete stochastic process-which allows for an information-theoretic analysis-can provide new insights into the behavior of probabilistic language generators, for example, why high-probability texts can be dull or repetitive.

method.value: Today's probabilistic language generators fall short when it comes to producing coherent and fluent text despite the fact that the underlying models perform well under standard metrics (e.

data_or_scenario.value: Perhaps surprisingly, for many language generation tasks, decoding strategies that aim to find the highest-probability strings produce text that is undesirable (Holtzman et al.

main_conclusion.value: Automatic and human evaluations show that, in comparison to nucleus and top-k sampling, locally typical sampling offers competitive performance (in both abstractive summarization and story generation) in terms of quality while consistently reducing degenerate repetitions.

limitations.value: However, stochastic strategies still have a host of other problems, while not entirely dispensing with those seen in maximization-based approaches.

relevance_strength.value: In this work, we posit that the abstraction of natural language generation as a discrete stochastic process-which allows for an information-theoretic analysis-can provide new insights into the behavior of probabilistic language generators, for example, why high-probability texts can be dull or repetitive.
